evidence_quotes.quote: it asks the car manufacturer for permission to start before turning on.
a button which some company decided to press just so they could artificially inflate their sales and margins...

paragraphs.text: Imagine you buy a car: the car dealership gives you the keys to your brand new vehicle and everything works perfectly, only that each times you try to start the car, it asks the car manufacturer for permission to start before turning on. The car works great otherwise and you can enjoy it to it's fullest, until one day, with only a short delay warning, the car manufacturer decides that your model of car is not "financially viable" and they will thus abandon the model in favor of their newest one. Your car is still there, as well as the keys you received along with it when you bought the car, but when you insert and turn the key: the car just doesn't react, it doesn't show any sign of life other than a message from the manufacturer inviting you to buy their latest model of cars. It's still in your garage with your set of keys, but it was instantly turned into scrap metal by a simple press of a button, a button which some company decided to press just so they could artificially inflate their sales and margins... I do not want cars to work this way, and I'm sure you don't either, so why should we allow digital goods to work in this exact same way?
